title: How CrossFit Champion Kelly Abney Built Her Athletic Empire While Breaking Barriers
guestBio: Kelly Abney is a CrossFit Games champion and founder of Abney Athletics. She's built a powerful presence in both competitive fitness and business, breaking barriers for women in athletics while creating her own empire.
excerpt: CrossFit Games champion Kelly Abney shares her journey of building an athletic empire while navigating the unique challenges women face in fitness. Discover how grit, balance, and owning your strength can transform your approach to leadership.
metaDescription: CrossFit champion Kelly Abney reveals how she built her athletic empire, overcame barriers in fitness, and balanced competition with business growth.
keyTakeaways: Success in athletics and business requires embracing both your strength and vulnerability as a woman.
The unseen hustle behind women's achievements often involves navigating unique challenges that men don't face.
Building an empire means creating systems that support your values while scaling your impact.
Grit isn't just about pushing through—it's about strategic persistence aligned with your goals.
Breaking barriers requires you to own your space unapologetically while lifting others up.
Balance isn't about perfection—it's about intentional choices that align with your priorities.
Your strength as a woman isn't something to minimize—it's your competitive advantage.

pullQuotes.text: Your strength as a woman isn't something to minimize—it's your competitive advantage.
Building an empire means creating systems that support your values while scaling your impact.

timestamps.label: Kelly's CrossFit journey and championship wins
The unseen challenges of women in athletics
Building Abney Athletics from the ground up
Breaking barriers and owning your strength

content:
When you think about building an empire, you might picture boardrooms and corporate ladders. But what if I told you that some of the most powerful leadership lessons come from the gym floor? In this episode of The Manage Her, we sit down with Kelly Abney, CrossFit Games champion and founder of Abney Athletics, who's redefining what it means to be a woman in fitness while building a business that's changing lives.

Kelly's story isn't just about lifting heavy weights or winning championships—though she's done plenty of both. It's about the grit, determination, and strategic thinking that goes into creating something meaningful while navigating an industry that hasn't always made room for women to truly shine. Her journey offers invaluable insights for any woman looking to build something significant, whether that's in athletics, business, or any field where you're ready to break barriers.

The Unseen Hustle of Women in Athletics

One of the most powerful aspects of Kelly's story is her willingness to talk about the unseen hustle that women face in athletics. While everyone sees the victories and the podium moments, there's an entire layer of challenges that women navigate that often goes unnoticed. From dealing with different recovery needs to managing the mental game differently than their male counterparts, female athletes are constantly adapting and innovating in ways that require incredible resilience.

Kelly shares how she learned to embrace these differences rather than see them as obstacles. Instead of trying to train exactly like the men around her, she developed her own approach that honored her body's needs while still pushing her to elite performance levels. This mindset shift—from seeing differences as disadvantages to viewing them as unique strengths—is something that translates directly to the business world and leadership in general.

The lesson here is profound: you don't need to succeed despite being a woman. You can succeed because of the unique perspective, resilience, and approach that you bring as a woman. Kelly's championship wins weren't achieved by trying to be like everyone else—they came from owning exactly who she was and maximizing her strengths.

Building Systems That Scale Your Impact

Transitioning from being an elite athlete to building Abney Athletics required Kelly to think like an entrepreneur while maintaining her competitive edge. She discovered that the same principles that made her successful in CrossFit—consistency, strategic planning, and relentless focus on improvement—were exactly what she needed to build a sustainable business.

But here's where Kelly's approach gets interesting: she didn't just create another fitness business. She built systems that could scale her impact beyond what she could achieve as an individual athlete. Through Abney Athletics, she's able to influence and empower far more people than she ever could through her personal training alone. This shift from individual achievement to scalable impact is a crucial evolution that every leader needs to understand.

Kelly talks about the importance of creating frameworks that work even when you're not directly involved in every aspect. As someone who's still competing at the highest levels while running a growing business, she's had to learn how to build processes that maintain quality and deliver results without requiring her constant hands-on involvement. This is empire-building 101: creating systems that amplify your impact rather than just multiplying your workload.

The Strategic Side of Grit

When we talk about grit, it's easy to think of it as just pushing through obstacles with sheer willpower. But Kelly's definition of grit goes much deeper. She describes it as strategic persistence—the ability to stay committed to your goals while being flexible about your methods. This nuanced understanding of grit has been crucial to both her athletic success and her business growth.

In CrossFit, Kelly learned that sometimes grit means knowing when to push harder, and sometimes it means knowing when to pull back and recover. The same principle applies to building a business. There are moments when you need to double down and push through challenges, and there are moments when you need to step back, reassess, and adjust your strategy. True grit isn't about being stubborn—it's about being strategically persistent in pursuit of your bigger vision.

This approach has allowed Kelly to maintain her competitive edge while building something sustainable. She's not burning out or sacrificing her long-term success for short-term gains. Instead, she's playing a longer game that honors both her immediate goals and her larger vision for what she wants to create.

Owning Your Space Unapologetically

Perhaps the most powerful theme in Kelly's story is her journey to owning her space unapologetically. In a world that often asks women to be smaller, quieter, or more accommodating, Kelly has learned to take up the space she deserves—both in the gym and in the business world. This isn't about being aggressive or dominating others; it's about refusing to diminish yourself to make others comfortable.

Kelly shares how she had to unlearn the tendency to downplay her achievements or apologize for her success. Instead, she's embraced the idea that her visibility and success create opportunities for other women to see what's possible. By owning her space unapologetically, she's not just advancing her own career—she's expanding what other women believe is possible for themselves.

This mindset shift is crucial for any woman building something significant. You're not doing anyone any favors by playing small or hiding your capabilities. When you own your space and showcase your strengths, you create a ripple effect that benefits everyone around you. Kelly's success in both athletics and business serves as a powerful example of what becomes possible when you refuse to limit yourself based on others' expectations.

What's Next for the Empire

As Kelly looks toward the future, her vision extends far beyond personal achievements. She's thinking about legacy, impact, and the kind of change she wants to create in the world. Abney Athletics is just the beginning of what she sees as a larger mission to transform how women approach fitness, competition, and success in general.

The conversation reveals that Kelly's next moves are focused on scaling her impact even further while maintaining the authentic connection and high standards that have made her successful so far. She's not interested in growth for growth's sake—she wants expansion that aligns with her values and amplifies the positive change she's creating.

Kelly Abney's story is a masterclass in building something meaningful while staying true to who you are. Her journey from CrossFit champion to business owner illustrates that the same qualities that make you successful in one area—grit, strategic thinking, and authentic leadership—can be leveraged to create success in entirely different arenas. Most importantly, her example shows that you don't have to choose between being strong and being successful. In fact, your strength might just be the key to everything you want to build.

quiz.title: What's Your Empire-Building Style?
quiz.description: Discover how you naturally approach building something meaningful in your life, inspired by Kelly Abney's journey from CrossFit champion to business owner.

questions.question: When you're facing a major challenge in your career or personal life, what's your first instinct?

options.text: Analyze the situation thoroughly and create a step-by-step plan to tackle it
Reach out to your network for advice and see how you can support each other
Trust your gut and look for a creative solution that feels right for you
Take a step back, breathe, and adapt your approach based on what you've learned before

questions.question: You're in a room full of people who seem to 'get' something that you don't quite understand yet. How do you handle it?

options.text: Ask strategic questions to quickly get up to speed and find your angle
Find someone who seems approachable and start building a genuine connection
Listen carefully and contribute your own unique perspective when it feels natural
Observe first, then gradually engage as you understand the dynamics better

questions.question: When you think about your biggest professional accomplishment, what made it meaningful?

options.text: You proved something important to yourself and showed what was possible
It created opportunities or inspiration for other people you care about
You stayed true to your values and did it in a way that felt authentically you
You overcame significant obstacles and grew stronger through the process

questions.question: Your approach to setting boundaries at work or in business tends to be:

options.text: Clear and direct—you communicate your limits confidently and stick to them
Collaborative—you work with others to find solutions that respect everyone's needs
Values-based—you make decisions based on what aligns with your core beliefs
Flexible but firm—you adapt to situations while protecting your essential priorities

questions.question: When someone questions your capabilities or approach, your typical response is:

options.text: Let your results do the talking and use it as motivation to excel even more
Stay focused on the bigger picture and the positive impact you're trying to make
Trust yourself and remember that being different doesn't mean being wrong
Learn what you can from the feedback while staying committed to your path

questions.question: If you were building something important from the ground up, your priority would be:

options.text: Creating systems and strategies that could scale and create lasting impact
Building a culture and community that supports everyone involved
Ensuring everything you build reflects your authentic vision and values
Developing something sustainable that can weather challenges and grow over time

results.title: The Strategic Powerhouse
results.description: You approach challenges with calculated intensity and aren't afraid to stand out from the crowd. Like Kelly, you understand that your unique perspective as a woman isn't something to minimize—it's your competitive advantage. You naturally think several moves ahead and create systems that support your long-term vision. This episode will inspire you to embrace your strategic mindset even more boldly and show you how to turn your differences into unstoppable strengths.

results.title: The Community Builder
results.description: Your superpower lies in lifting others while you climb, creating meaningful connections that fuel both personal and professional growth. You instinctively understand that true success means breaking barriers for yourself and others. Like Kelly's approach to the fitness industry, you're changing the game by making space for everyone to thrive. Kelly's story will resonate with your values and give you practical insights on scaling your impact while staying true to your collaborative nature.

results.title: The Authentic Innovator
results.description: You succeed by staying true to yourself and finding creative solutions that honor your unique needs and values. Rather than following traditional paths, you carve out approaches that work specifically for you. This mirrors Kelly's journey of training differently than her male counterparts and building a business that reflects her values. Her story will validate your instinct to do things your own way and show you how authenticity can be your greatest business asset.

results.title: The Resilient Adapter
results.description: You excel at navigating challenges with grace, turning obstacles into opportunities through strategic persistence. Your strength comes from understanding that balance isn't about perfection—it's about making intentional choices aligned with your priorities. Kelly's journey through the unseen struggles of female athletics while building her empire will speak directly to your experience of managing multiple demands. This episode will give you fresh perspectives on sustainable success and strategic resilience.

transcript:
**Aimee:** Hey everyone, I'm your host Aimee Rickabus, author of The Manager: Unveiling the Invisible Work and Sparking a Leadership Revolution, hitting Amazon May 1st, 2025. I'm the CEO of Tomahawk Information Solutions, an awesome IT crew pulling in 8 figures, and a serial entrepreneur since I was 22. Think real estate flips, prenatal protein powder, and film production. I'm a mom of six and this is the Manager podcast where we're digging into the unseen hustle and sparking some serious leadership vibes.

I'm stoked to introduce Kelly Abney, the powerhouse behind Abney Athletics and a CrossFit Games winner placing seventh place. Kelly's a beast in the gym, coaching and crushing it all the while juggling her own crew. So, Kelly, I'm so happy to have you.

**Kelly:** Oh my god. Thank you so much.

**Aimee:** Kelly's also my neighbor and so we've been in the trenches with our little kids chasing people around all day.

**Kelly:** Exactly. Yes, ma'am. We've got our tribe of children in the neighborhood. For sure. We do. Thank you for having me on here. Absolutely thrilled to be here.

**Aimee:** I'm so happy to have you. And so look, you got a chance to look at the book.

**Kelly:** I did. It's amazing. I mean, I'm not surprised that you would write something as inspirational as you have. I mean, we've watched you with six kids. I have two and I'm like, "Okay, I'm tired." And you're over there managing a business, running a house, getting everybody everywhere. I mean, the amount of times I watch you go in and out of that cul-de-sac.

**Aimee:** Oh my gosh. I live in my car. There's a whole lot of waving as we go by each other. Like, here we go.

**Kelly:** So, I'm excited for you. It's awesome.

**Aimee:** Thank you. So in chapter one that you got to read, I was talking about the invisible work that women carry for society - in our homes, in business. How does that show up for you?

**Kelly:** I mean I think as women and moms and business women in general, we are always doing invisible work. It's never if you sit down and you say I have five minutes, you don't have five minutes. You forgot to do something. Or you are thinking what do I have to do next? 

The women I know, my clients, everybody in my life - I was up at 2 a.m. thinking about the list of things I have to do today. It rarely does my husband have that same conversation with me. I think we are constantly doing work to get ahead to figure out what our next step is. So invisible work I think for us is just a constant and we don't ever think about how much we do.

**Aimee:** We don't and we just do it. I didn't think about it until my therapist asked me to write down everything I do for my household.

**Kelly:** Oh yeah.

**Aimee:** And I was like, I don't even think that's a possibility. She's like, I want you to write down everything you do and then I want your husband to write down everything he does. And I was like, how about we just write down what he does and then everything else that's mine.

**Kelly:** You're like, you have a spiral book and a sheet of paper. That's how that's going to work for us.

**Aimee:** That's kind of what sparked me on this journey of unveiling the invisible work for me because I didn't really realize how having two little babies and going through Covid and all of a sudden I'm homeschooling all these other kids and running the business and all of these things happening all at once and I didn't realize why I was feeling so burnt out. And then once I kind of named it, I was like, "Oh, this makes sense." Once I stopped gaslighting myself on the fact that I was actually just doing too much, doing a lot, taking on a lot - not just groceries and laundry and what's for dinner and "mom did you buy that book for me for my English class?" I'm like, oh my gosh.

**Kelly:** Dress up day tomorrow. Yes. Good luck. I don't even know what to do with them. It's 80s day tomorrow. It's the neighborhood text. Does anybody have an 80s outfit?

**Aimee:** Exactly. Thank god we have each other. Oh my gosh. I mean, we are the seaside sisterhood. Our neighborhood is a rare gem in this age to have a neighborhood where we get together for Bunco once a month, where we do pumpkin carving. We get together to stamp the streets for bunny stomping for Easter coming up. We do a chili cook off and we don't have an association. This isn't something - this was like, this is honestly Jenny. It is a lot of Jenny. I think it is.

But I think the thing that I love the most about our neighborhood is that we create time for us as women and moms and wives and we create time for all of us to hang out because we all know what you're talking about, being burnt out.

**Kelly:** I think the thing that I've discovered as I've gotten older is the thing that gives me energy besides work I do, which we just talked about - when you're passionate about something, you get this renewed energy coming out of it. When I'm coaching a team or when I have a really amazing client that's doing really well. You kind of come out of that rather than feeling drained, you're re-energized.

So, I think that when we all can get together and create these times for us to do this, whatever it is, gives all of us just a little bit of a breath of fresh air and then we can talk about our frustrations like, "Oh god, I'm not the only person going through this." I think it's just such a safe place for us and we get to walk right down the door to do it. So, I don't have to get on a plane or get in a car or FaceTime anybody. I just get to hang out with you guys which is great. And I think it works because we do create that time and that space for us to have that community.

**Aimee:** So it's like it seems to be this recurring theme - if we have the space and the time to gather we will.

**Kelly:** And people will reach out - are you doing okay? Do you need help? Is there something I can get you? There's a lot of that conversation. So it's a constant check-in with each other. And if there's space that needs to be created because somebody's lost somebody or a kid's sick or something like that. We've gone through it. We've been in it. We really have.

**Aimee:** It really is. Like coming over and making me food when I'm recovering from baby Paul. The sisterhood is real. It really is. I think sisterhood is what feeds our souls as women and I don't have real sisters - I have a brother so finding that grown-up tribe, your lady tribe.

**Kelly:** It's true and it doesn't happen till later. You think you have it when you go through high school and then you have it in college and then you have it in your 20s and all of a sudden you're like no none of this is what I thought it was and then you find this group of women when you get older. And it's something that you didn't even know you needed and now you have it and it's an organic development that you just found yourself in and you're like thank god because I'm tired of everything else.

**Aimee:** I had a feeling when we were looking for a house and I was driving and we looked at so many neighborhoods and actually we were in escrow in a couple of different houses. We could have ended up somewhere else. And when that house came on the market, I was like, "Look at all the kids playing in the streets." Look, people wave to each other in this cul-de-sac. I actually could tell there was this culture of our little - it's just a couple of streets. But we have this little culture in these couple of blocks and I could tell this was special. 

And then when I moved in, the woman who was moving out of my house was like, "Okay, here's the deal. This is what we do. On Halloween, you need to make hot cocoa for our house." I haven't done it yet. That's okay. But there's tradition. There's a real tradition in the neighborhood. And it passes on from neighbor to neighbor as the houses change hands. It stays.

**Kelly:** And the neighbors that we have there that have been there a while like Terry - they are so thankful. Every time we get a new neighbor, we're always really like, "Okay, are they going to fit with us? Is this going to be, can they get on board with us?" And so far so good.

**Aimee:** And I'm like that's one of the things - as our neighborhood is getting more expensive because the real estate is crazy. I mean, even since I moved in seven years ago - will having people who pay more for houses change our culture? I really hope that this idea expands into the world rather than getting lost completely.

**Kelly:** I mean, hopefully our kids take it if anything. Hopefully our kids are like, "This is how I grew up and my mom had this social circle in our community and my dad saw this, too." And hopefully they continue it because that really is - we're looking for the next generations to carry these on.

**Aimee:** Step up and step in. For sure. So, I'm curious - I'm an IT girl and just curious how you are in your AI journey? Where are you at?

**Kelly:** It's interesting because we were just chatting before and I feel like I am a little bit behind when it comes to it because a lot of what I do in my job is so hands-on. Not to say that technology shouldn't be there because it should, right? I just feel like I tend to get stuck in my old ways and then somebody will show me something and I never even thought about that.

So, I'm very old school. I write my client list, my bookings for the week. I write it out by hand. It's pencil and paper. And then I really just don't use as much as I should, right? But I do use an app called Train Heroic, and that's what I remote program my clients for.

**Aimee:** Oh, that's cool.

**Kelly:** So, they can get in and they log in and I can see their progress, ask questions. There's videos. It's a really neat app. So, I've started using that with clients that aren't in my gym. And then we have all the regular social media platforms. So, all of those get my face out or whatever information I'm trying to get out there. I do tend to use more social media platforms than anything else.

**Aimee:** I love that. But there's so many options. That's the very tip of an iceberg that goes under. It's funny. I feel like I in a lot of ways I've been able to see how digital transformation changed the Fortune 500, right? And so for me, it's kind of - I feel like a digital transformation consultant for women - how can you guys embrace automation and increase the amount of time that you have in your life and actually become more efficient, maybe even save some money.

And so it's funny, but as I was writing this book, that was part of the thing I was thinking about - as women in our 40s, sometimes we have a tendency to not be early adopters.

**Kelly:** Totally right. We watch our parents do it, too. We're like, we'll never do this. No, no, I'm kind of doing it. Kind of happening right now.

**Aimee:** Ride the wave, baby. Ride the wave. So, it's like I would love to be able to be there for women and be like, "Yeah, no, embrace AI." Reach out to friends that are doing it. Talk to your kids about it because your kids know way more than we do about this stuff. And I think that as women, we tend to learn from each other more than - I feel like men like to research.

**Kelly:** Yes. My husband will look at something and he's like I want to buy this, but I'm going to look at 300 sites and I'm going to talk to 700 people and I'm like, okay, I'm going to talk to my girlfriends. And they're going to tell me what's happening. I mean, you can pretty much post it on the sisterhood. How many times do we see that? We're like, does anybody have a reference for this? A plumber. Does anybody know how to do this?

And I do think that we trust each other more than we might search it - once somebody gives us like, okay, well, let me look this up for a second. But we really do default to each other to tell me about this. So I think having somebody like you that is so savvy in that world - for somebody like me who gets overwhelmed, it's like people walk into the gym and they look at all this equipment and they think - that's me. I'm like, what do I do with this? Not my wheelhouse. For me, I'm like, oh, I have 300 new exercises for you. Don't worry about it.

So it's the same thing when it comes to IT. For you, I'm looking at things. You're like, what are your apps you use? I'm like, you tell me because I'm trying to work smarter, not harder now. So, I want you because this is your wheelhouse. Tell me what it is I should be doing. And then I'm going to follow it because once you find your people and you know that they're good at what they do. I'm a big advocate for I will hire you because you're good at what you do and you are going to tell me how to do this.

**Aimee:** Oh, totally. I'm a delegator myself. I don't want to learn. I don't want to reinvent the wheel. I don't have time for that. I'm looking for the quickest way from point A to point B at every moment of the day.

**Kelly:** And I think that that's part of what your book is about, right? Us learning how to do things more efficiently because as women, we don't really shy away from something that's hard, right? Something's hard, we're like, "Okay, well, here we go." Let's grit down and we're going to finish this and we're going to figure it out and okay, we have a 140 degree fever, but we're getting everybody everywhere they need to go today. Mom doesn't get sick. We just do it.

And I think that when people introduce things to us that are going to help us minimize the amount of time that we have to give to whatever project it is, right? We're like, "Thank God. Where's this been my whole life?"

**Aimee:** Exactly. I mean, I spent eight hours one day writing a proposal for the gas and oil industry about digital transformation and how it could save them money and time and automation and I mean, this whole thing. Then the next morning I woke up and I was like you know what I'm going to try this AI thing and I downloaded the Genius app and I basically gave it the parameters, told it basically what I had done the day before and instantly it generated something even better than what I had spent 8 hours on the day before and I realized you know what that's 8 hours I could have spent with my newborn baby.

**Kelly:** Hello. Do I have other things that I'd rather be doing? Would I rather be at the park with my kids? Always. For sure. My priorities as a mom. If I can save a little time or a lot of time, why wouldn't I?

**Aimee:** And that was my real aha moment for myself realizing that I needed to embrace digital transformation just the way that the corporations were embracing digital transformation. I was like, "Wow, holy crap." Once I stopped gaslighting myself on the fact that I was actually just doing too much, doing a lot, taking on a lot - not just groceries and laundry and what's for dinner and "mom did you buy that book for me for my English class?" I'm like, oh my gosh. Dress up day tomorrow.

**Kelly:** Yes. Good luck. I don't even know what to do with them. It's 80s day tomorrow. It's the neighborhood text. Does anybody have an 80s outfit?

**Aimee:** Exactly. Thank god we have each other. And every woman I talk to who's embracing AI is using it a little differently. So I feel like I'm getting tips from all of my guests - everybody has been using it in some other way or using some other technology platform and I never would have thought of that that's amazing and that's why we need those people and having conversations about technology as women. I feel like technology has always been such the man's world.

**Kelly:** It very much has been and I don't know why because when you think about how many things we do a day that involves technology, right? I just think that it's kind of a world that our moms didn't really dive into that much. At least my mom wasn't real tech savvy.

**Aimee:** I was a little lucky there. My mom was - if anything that they told my mom was not a girl thing was a thing that she wanted to do. She was into it. She's like, "This is it. I'm doing this." She's defiant that way.

**Kelly:** Well, that makes sense. Look at you. That's why you're a CEO of an IT company. That makes complete sense.

**Aimee:** Totally. So, I got lucky there, but I did see it in my tribe of mothers. I was raised in a tribe of mothers. I really was. And in my tribe of mothers, most of the mothers were stay-at-home moms, and most of them didn't embrace technology and still don't embrace technology. And I don't know what it is. I think that it takes people like you who say to us, listen, we can make this easier for you, right? Like, let me show you how to do this.

**Kelly:** And I think it makes a difference when it comes from somebody who's doing the same thing we're doing. You're just doing it more efficiently. Right. It's not like, oh, I'm not married and I have no kids, right? I mean, I'm going to listen to you and I'll filter through what I want. I always tell people, tell me whatever you want to tell me. I can filter through that whether I'm going to use it or not. But as a mom of six working and running a house and then you're like let me tell you how to make this easier. I'm going to listen to you. Because your world is busier than my world. And if you're making it easier for you, I want it easier for me, too.

**Aimee:** Totally. My mom always said, "Aimee, you run that house like a general." And I'm like, "Thanks, Mom." But I think it's part of bringing that CEO mentality into my house and trying to run it with the same kind of systems that you run your companies. That's part of the Manager, too - how can I teach you those kinds of skills, too? Those leadership and management skills that we do in our house all the time - organizing our kids calendars and their schedules and the husband's doctor's appointments and all of the things. Life is a series of managing and organizing all the things.

**Kelly:** It's interesting because I think that we tend to look to things like I got to teach my kid how to cook, right? So there are certain skills that we look at and we think I got to teach you how to do laundry. I need to teach you how to drive. I need to teach you how to do these things. But what we don't realize is that there's the day-to-day stuff that we do that we also need to teach our kids. So watching us manage a house I think is a skill that all of our kids should be able to look at and say I'm going to take something from this.

**Aimee:** I hope so. And also delegating some of the tasks to the kids - laundry. These are the real things. It's like are you really going to use geometry? But are you really going to need to know how to be proficient at washing your dishes and how to do laundry properly? I mean, doing laundry right can save you a lot of money because you're not going to ruin your clothes. If you're going to show up to an interview, you probably need clothes to wear. And you don't want them to be pink because you put the red in with the white.

**Kelly:** Super short because you put it on high.

**Aimee:** But like honestly, it's the skills. I do feel like delegating some of the invisible work that we typically have taken on in our households as women and delegating some of that to our children is actually doing them a service because we're going to give them life skills, real life things. Either way, I mean, they're going to have to learn all that stuff. It's the things that we don't necessarily look at at that list - these are things that I'm glad that I learned and a lot of it I learned through sports, too.

So, I think that the activities that we put our kids in - running around to seven different stops a day is exhausting, but then you look at what's the other option, right? You guys are going to sit at home and not do anything. Well, no, you need to learn how to play on a team. You need to learn how to talk to adults. You need to learn how to order your own food. There's so many things that we do when we take them out that are just skills. And you will meet kids that don't have those that are very sheltered.

**Aimee:** Oh, yeah. And you're just - it's painful. I don't even know how you're going to do life. You might be book smart. You are going to nail that test. But how are you going to function in the actual world and talk to actual people?

**Kelly:** So all that invisible work that you talk about that we do is really what keeps life moving. And if you don't have that, then you know, well, okay, I guess you did one skill today. That's spectacular, right? We missed everything else today.

**Aimee:** Totally. I couldn't agree more. It's definitely the invisible work that keeps the world moving. And now if you have an app that's going to make my invisible work easier. Please sign me up.

**Kelly:** Oh my gosh. Yeah.

**Aimee:** Well, you've got to get into AI. Honestly, Grok, dear. It's free right now.
